method3.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
The Hunga volcano is located in the Pacific Ocean along the Tonga segment of the Tonga-Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, a massive 2,550-kilometer subduction system formed where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The volcano is situated approximately 65 kilometers north of Tongatapu, Tonga's main island, and 30 kilometers south of the Fonuafoʻou submarine volcano.[5] Rising more than 1.8 kilometers high and extending about 20 kilometers across the seafloor, the volcanic structure is mostly submerged.[4] Its eruptive products are primarily andesitic in composition, which is typical for intra-oceanic arcs, and consist of alternating layers of andesitic lava flows, porous scoria, lapilli, and ash.[2][4]

At the summit of the volcano is a large submarine caldera measuring approximately 5 kilometers in diameter.[4] High-resolution multibeam sonar surveys have revealed a 4 by 2 kilometer depression that is 150 meters deep, which was formed by the explosive collapse of an older volcanic structure during a previous eruption.[2] Historically, the only visible portions of this caldera were two small, uninhabited islands named Hunga Tonga to the north and Hunga Haʻapai to the west, which acted as the visible remnants of the caldera's ridge.[2][4] Following volcanic eruptions in 2014 and 2015, a large tephra cone was generated that connected the two islands into a single unified landmass named Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[2][4] This joined island covered an area of just over 1 square kilometer and reached a maximum altitude of 149 meters.[5] Scientists studied the morphological evolution and erosion of this newly formed island to better understand and recognize similar geological features on Mars.[4]

On January 15, 2022, the volcano experienced a massive eruption that was described as a once-in-1,000-years event for the Hunga caldera.[5] This event produced the largest underwater explosion ever recorded by modern scientific instruments and the biggest natural explosion in the geophysical record.[1][3] The eruption released an order of magnitude more energy than the 1980 eruption of Mount St. Helens.[3] The explosive force completely destroyed the unified island of Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai, blowing the landmass apart and leaving only very small fragments of the original islands emerging from the ocean at the eastern and western edges.[4][5]

==Geography==
Situated in the South Pacific, the volcano lies 65 kilometers north of Tongatapu, Tonga's main island, and about 30 kilometers south of the Fonuafoʻou submarine volcano.[2][4] Regionally, Fiji is located approximately 500 kilometers (310 miles) to the northwest, while New Zealand is between 1,600 and 2,000 kilometers (994 and 1,243 miles) away.[1] The broader volcanic structure rises more than 1.8 kilometers from the seafloor and spans about 20 kilometers across.[5] It exists within a 2,550-kilometer-long subduction system between Tonga and New Zealand, which is driven by the subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[5] 

Prior to the 2022 eruption, the unified island had a maximum altitude of 149 meters and covered an area of just over 1 square kilometer.[4] The remnants of this landmass are now confined to very small fragments located specifically on the western and eastern edges of the caldera.[4]

==Eruptions==
On January 15, 2022, at approximately 5:00 p.m. local time, the volcano erupted violently.[4] The initial explosion released an estimated 10 to 28 exajoules of energy, making it more powerful than the 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo and potentially comparable to the 1883 eruption of Krakatoa.[5] The blast produced a vertical plume of gas and ash that reached 36 miles (over 55 kilometers) into the atmosphere, setting a height record for the satellite era.[4][5] Scientists have unofficially classified the event as an "ultra Surtseyan" eruption.[3] While typical Surtseyan eruptions involve small amounts of water flashing to steam upon contacting magma, this event was driven by a partial collapse of the caldera's northern rim.[3] This structural failure allowed massive volumes of 20 °C seawater to rush into the confined, high-temperature magma chamber, triggering an exceptionally explosive reaction.[3]

The eruption generated massive tsunamis that swept across Tongan islands with waves reaching up to 65 feet above sea level.[4] The disaster destroyed hundreds of structures, displaced over 1,500 people, and caused four deaths in Tonga.[4] Tsunami waves also propagated across the Pacific Ocean, causing damage in Fiji, Hawaii, Chile, California, Japan, Russia, and New Zealand, as well as two deaths and an oil spill in Peru.[4] Beneath the ocean surface, the eruption displaced up to 1.66 cubic miles of material, creating deep ash ripples and gouged valleys up to 30 miles away from the volcano.[4] Tonga's domestic internet cable was severed during the event and buried under 100 feet of sediment and ash.[4] Because the nearest functioning seismic station was nearly 500 miles away, the United States Geological Survey had to develop new methods to detect and catalog the earthquake activity surrounding the volcano in the aftermath.[4]

Originating from a vent just 200 meters below the ocean surface, the submarine eruption injected unprecedented amounts of water vapor and volcanic aerosols into the stratosphere.[1][2] The event increased the total water content of the stratosphere by approximately 10 percent.[2] Because water vapor acts as a greenhouse gas and can linger for years, scientists initially feared the eruption could push global temperatures past the 1.5 °C warming threshold set by the Paris Accords.[1] However, the volcano also released a moderate amount of sulfur dioxide, which quickly converted into sulfate aerosols that reflect sunlight back into space.[1] Ultimately, the aerosols' cooling properties outweighed the warming influence of the water vapor, resulting in an overall slight cooling effect on the planet.[1] While a very slight warming influence was hinted at in the Northern Hemisphere, temperatures over the Southern Hemisphere were reduced by 0.1 °C.[1]

==Footnotes==
* The January 15, 2022, explosion was approximately seven times more powerful than a previous eruption that occurred at the volcano on December 20, 2021.[2]
* During the 2022 event, the volcano expelled 2.9 × 10¹⁵ grams of basaltic-andesitic volcanogenic material, with tephra covering an area at least 600 kilometers in diameter.[1]
* Geological evidence indicates that Tonga experienced a much larger tsunami with a 30-meter run-up height in the mid-15th century, which was likely caused by the collapse of an island volcano.[2]
* Following the 2022 eruption, a meteotsunami was observed as
